basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: al-Maʿārij — “the Ascending Stairways” — opens with a challenge: a questioner demands the promised chastisement. The answer comes from “God, Lord of the ascending stairways” (dhī l-maʿārij), which Qushayrī glosses: the Lord of grace and of the high stations to which He raises His saints. The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him “in a day whose measure is fifty thousand years.” The sūra commands the Prophet the “beautiful patience” (ṣabr jamīl), which Qushayrī defines: the one in which there is no complaint; in which the servant does not find patience heavy but tastes it as sweet; in which he does not await deliverance from it but remains still and content; which is in the contemplation of the One who afflicts. It describes man “created anxious” (halūʿ) — fretful in misfortune, niggardly in good fortune —, save the praying ones, those whose prayer is constant, whose wealth bears a recognized right for the beggar and the deprived, those who believe in the Day of Judgement and who guard their trusts. The sūra ends on the deniers rushing forward, necks craned, and the Day when they will come out of the graves as if racing toward their idols.
themes: the praying ones and their virtues

versets.trad: A questioner asked about a chastisement bound to fall
versets.commentaire: saʾala sāʾilun bi-ʿadhābin wāqiʿ (سَأَلَ سائِلٌ بِعَذابٍ واقِع): a questioner demanded an imminent chastisement. Qushayrī: the bi here has the sense of ʿan (about) — “a questioner inquired about this chastisement: for whom is it?” Tradition reports that a denier challenged: “if this is the truth from You, rain stones upon us!” A senseless challenge: to demand the chastisement one denies. But Qushayrī opens another reading: the verb could mean to invoke, and the questioner be the Prophet himself. The ambiguity leaves both faces open: the mockery of the unbeliever, or the supplication of the believer.

mots.racine: ع ذ ب / و ق ع
mots.def: ʿadhāb: chastisement; wāqiʿ: bound to fall.

versets.trad: upon the disbelievers, which none can repel,
versets.commentaire: laysa lahu dāfiʿ (لَيْسَ لَهُ دافِع): none can repel it. An answer to the question of verse 1: this chastisement is for the deniers, and none could ward it off. The challenge turns back: the one who demanded the chastisement learns that it is destined for him, and that once come, nothing will stop it. What one demands in bravado may befall one, and then no force repels it. The irresistibility of the chastisement speaks of the vanity of every challenge thrown at God.

versets.trad: from God, Lord of the ascending stairways.
versets.commentaire: dhī l-maʿārij (ذي المَعارِج): Lord of the ascending stairways. Qushayrī glosses magnificently: “Lord of grace (faḍl) and of the high stations (maʿālī al-darajāt) to which He raises His saints.” The word maʿārij — the ways of ascent — evokes not only the descent of chastisement, but the ascent of hearts toward God. The same God who lets the chastisement fall upon the deniers raises His friends by degrees. The title bears both faces: rigour for whoever shuts himself, elevation for whoever opens. The stairways go up or down according to the heart.

mots.def: maʿārij: ways of ascent, degrees of elevation.

versets.ar: تَعْرُجُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ وَالرُّوحُ إِلَيْهِ فِي يَوْمٍ كَانَ مِقْدَارُهُ خَمْسِينَ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ
versets.tr: taʿruju l-malāʾikatu wa-l-rūḥu ilayhi fī yawmin kāna miqdāruhu khamsīna alfa sanatin
versets.trad: The angels and the Spirit ascend to Him in a day whose measure is fifty thousand years.
versets.commentaire: taʿruju l-malāʾikatu wa-l-rūḥu ilayh (تَعْرُجُ المَلائِكَةُ وَالرّوحُ إلَيْه): the angels and the Spirit ascend to Him. al-Rūḥ: Gabriel, says Qushayrī. Fī yawmin… khamsīna alfa sana: in a day of fifty thousand years. Qushayrī gives several senses: the duration of the Day of Resurrection; or “He will judge the creatures in a brief day, a minimal time, which would take men fifty thousand years”; or the distance from the lowest to the highest of creatures, which the angels traverse in a day. The immensity of the number speaks of the transcendence of the divine measures: what utterly escapes us, God accomplishes in an instant.

mots.racine: ع ر ج / م ل ك / ر و ح
mots.sens: the angels and the Spirit ascend to Him
mots.def: ʿaraja: to ascend; al-Rūḥ: the Spirit (Gabriel).

mots.ar: فِي يَوْمٍ كَانَ مِقْدَارُهُ خَمْسِينَ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ
mots.tr: fī yawmin kāna miqdāruhu khamsīna alfa sana
mots.sens: in a day of fifty thousand years

versets.trad: So be patient with a beautiful patience!
versets.commentaire: ṣabran jamīlan (صَبْرًا جَميلًا): a beautiful patience. A pearl of Qushayrī, who multiplies its definitions: “be patient, O Muhammad, in bearing their harm with a beautiful patience. The beautiful patience is the one in which there is no complaint; the one in which you do not find patience heavy, but taste it as sweet; the one in which the servant does not await deliverance from it, but remains still and content (sākin rāḍī); the one which is in the contemplation of the One who afflicts (shuhūd al-mublī); the one stripped of all complaint and all claim.” Beautiful patience is not gritting one's teeth while awaiting the end; it is consenting from the heart, looking toward the One who afflicts rather than toward the affliction.

mots.racine: ص ب ر / ج م ل

versets.trad: They see it as far off,
versets.commentaire: yarawnahu baʿīdan (يَرَوْنَهُ بَعيدًا): they see it far off. Qushayrī: “one judges a thing far off only because one doubts it; but the one who is certain of a thing does not judge it distant.” The deniers think the Day far off — proof that deep down they do not believe in it. The distance they ascribe to it is not in the Day but in their doubt. What one holds to be far off is what one is not sure of. Conversely, whoever is certain of the return feels it near, and lives accordingly.

versets.trad: while We see it near.
versets.commentaire: wa-narāhu qarīban (وَنَراهُ قَريبًا): We see it near. Qushayrī: “what is coming is near; one judges far off only by doubt.” A sharp contrast: what the deniers think far off (v. 6), God sees near. In God's sight, for whom time is nothing, the Day is imminent. Nearness is the truth, distance the illusion of doubt. To live in the certainty that the Day is near is to see as God sees; to push it off into the distance is to blind oneself by doubt. The wise hold near what the heedless think far.

mots.racine: ر أ ي / ق ر ب

versets.trad: The Day when the heaven will be like molten metal
versets.commentaire: ka-l-muhl (كَالمُهْل): like molten metal, or the dregs of oil. The heaven, a solid vault, melts like metal. Qushayrī draws the inner lesson: “that Day, the one who was in the height of his arrogance and the surge of his domination softens and is humbled; the proud grows weak, the lofty is brought low.” The melting of the heaven is an image of the collapse of all pride: what was hard and high becomes soft and low. On the Day, pride melts like metal in the fire; only humility remains.

mots.racine: ك و ن / س م و
mots.sens: the day the heaven will be

versets.trad: and the mountains like tufts of wool.
versets.commentaire: ka-l-ʿihn (كَالعِهْن): like carded wool, tufts. The mountains — symbols of mass and firmness — become light as tufts of wool the wind carries off. A striking reversal: what was heaviest becomes lightest. On the Day, the weight of the world has no weight. As the heaven melts (v. 8), the mountains fly off: all the solid dissolves. What man leaned upon — mass, stability — proves as insubstantial as a tuft. Only what truly weighs remains: the works of the heart.

mots.racine: ك و ن / ج ب ل

versets.trad: No devoted friend will ask after his friend
versets.commentaire: lā yasʾalu ḥamīmun ḥamīman (لا يَسْأَلُ حَميمٌ حَميمًا): no intimate inquires after his intimate. Qushayrī: “no kin turns to a kin, for each, on that Day, has an affair that absorbs him entirely; and none takes care of the poor, on that Day, but God.” On the Day, human bonds dissolve in the urgency of one's own fate: each is too overwhelmed by his own account to attend to another. The solitude is total. But Qushayrī lets a gleam break through: if no friend inquires, God Himself takes care of the forsaken. When all human aid is lacking, God's help remains.

mots.racine: س أ ل / ح م م
mots.sens: no intimate inquires after an intimate

versets.ar: يُبَصَّرُونَهُمْ يَوَدُّ الْمُجْرِمُ لَوْ يَفْتَدِي مِنْ عَذَابِ يَوْمِئِذٍ بِبَنِيهِ
versets.tr: yubaṣṣarūnahum yawaddu l-mujrimu law yaftadī min ʿadhābi yawmiʾidhin bi-banīhi
versets.trad: though they will be made to see one another. The guilty one would wish to ransom himself from that Day's chastisement with his sons,
versets.commentaire: yawaddu l-mujrimu law yaftadī… bi-banīh (يَوَدُّ المُجْرِمُ لَوْ يَفْتَدي بِبَنيه): the guilty would wish to ransom himself with his sons. Qushayrī: “they recognize one another, but no heart softens toward another.” The height of despair: the guilty one would hand over his own children to escape the chastisement. What he cherished most, he would abandon. The ransom speaks of extreme panic: one would give everything, even one's own, to save oneself. But this bargain is vain: nothing ransoms on the Day what one has not prepared. The strongest attachment yields before terror, and yet avails nothing.

mots.sens: they are made to see them

mots.racine: و د د / ف د ي
mots.sens: the guilty would wish to ransom himself
mots.def: wadda: to wish; iftadā: to ransom.

mots.racine: ع ذ ب / ب ن ي
mots.sens: from that Day's chastisement, with his sons

versets.trad: his wife, his brother,
versets.commentaire: wa-ṣāḥibatihi wa-akhīh (وَصاحِبَتِهِ وَأَخيه): his wife and his brother. The list of those he would hand over lengthens: after the sons, the wife, the brother. All the bonds of blood and heart are cast into the scale of impossible ransom. The guilty one, who perhaps lived only for his own, would abandon them all to save himself. It is the collapse of all earthly attachments before the terror of the Day. What one set above God proves, at the hour of the account, of no ransoming worth: one would give it for nothing.

versets.trad: his clan that sheltered him
versets.commentaire: faṣīlatihi llatī tuʾwīh (فَصيلَتِهِ الَّتي تُؤْويه): his clan that sheltered him. He would hand over even his tribe, the very one that sheltered and protected him. The clan was, in ancient Arabia, the supreme security, the rampart against all dangers. And yet the guilty one would abandon it. All the supports of the world — family, clan, alliances — are cast in vain into the scale. On the Day, the only refuge is in God; all the others, which one preferred, cannot ransom, and would be handed over without hesitation.

versets.ar: وَمَن فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعًا ثُمَّ يُنجِيهِ
versets.tr: wa-man fī l-arḍi jamīʿan thumma yunjīhi
versets.trad: and all who are on the earth, that it might then save him.
versets.commentaire: wa-man fī l-arḍi jamīʿan thumma yunjīh (وَمَنْ في الأَرْضِ جَميعًا ثُمَّ يُنْجيه): and all on the earth, that it might at last save him. The height of the escalation: he would give the whole of humanity to ransom himself. But the “that it might save him” hangs suspended — for none of this will save him. The list rises from sons to the whole world, and ends in the void: no ransom is possible. On the Day, salvation is not bought; it depends solely on what one has prepared. To give the entire universe ransoms not one ounce of the deserved chastisement. Only what one sent ahead saves.

mots.racine: أ ر ض / ج م ع
mots.sens: and all who are on the earth

versets.trad: By no means!... It is the Blaze:
versets.commentaire: kallā innahā laẓā (كَلّا إنَّها لَظى): by no means, it is Laẓā. The kallā cuts off: no ransom is possible. Laẓā — the devouring Flame — is one of the names of the Fire. After the desperate listing of ransoms (v. 11-14), the answer is final: nothing ransoms. The guilty one strikes the wall of the irreversible. What he should have prepared in life cannot be bought on the Day at any price. The Flame awaits, and no bargain turns it aside.

mots.def: Laẓā: name of the Fire, the blazing flame.

versets.trad: eager to scorch,
versets.commentaire: nazzāʿatan li-l-shawā (نَزّاعَةً لِلشَّوى): tearing off the skin of the scalp and extremities. Qushayrī: “tearer of the limbs; it strips the skin from the face and the bone.” The shawā denotes the scalp, the extremities, all that is not vital. An image of the devouring intensity of the Fire. But Qushayrī suggests, in the background, that the chastisement does not kill: it rages without finishing, abiding forever. The description, terrible, is not made for the pleasure of horror, but to give its true weight to the present choice.

versets.trad: it calls the one who turned his back and went away,
versets.commentaire: tadʿū man adbara wa-tawallā (تَدْعو مَنْ أَدْبَرَ وَتَوَلّى): it calls the one who turned his back and turned away. The Fire, personified, calls toward itself those who turned away from God. Qushayrī opens a striking inner reading: “the gehenna of this world clings to a man's heart: it calls him, with the hooks of greed (kalālīb al-ḥirṣ), toward itself, and drags him to amass, until he prefers the world to himself and to everyone.” The fire of the hereafter calls whoever turned away; the fire of greed calls already in this world. To turn away from God is to let oneself be hooked by the world.

mots.racine: د ب ر / و ل ي
mots.sens: the one who turned his back and went away
mots.def: adbara: to turn the back; tawallā: to turn away.

versets.trad: who amassed and hoarded.
versets.commentaire: wa-jamaʿa fa-awʿā (وَجَمَعَ فَأَوْعى): who amassed and hoarded. Here is the portrait of the one the Fire calls: the amasser, the hoarder. Qushayrī continues his reading: hooked by the hooks of greed, he “prefers the world to himself and to all others, to the point of being miserly with his fortune toward his own children and kin; and few escape the ruse of the world and its seductions.” To amass without giving, to keep for oneself alone, is to answer the call of the inner fire. Avarice is already a gehenna: it devours the heart before the Fire devours the body.

versets.trad: Truly, man was created anxious,
versets.commentaire: khuliqa halūʿan (خُلِقَ هَلوعًا): created anxious, greedy and impatient. Qushayrī: “the halaʿ is intense greed joined to anxiety (jazaʿ)”; “tossed in the floods of cravings”; “the little contents him, the little angers him”; “in affliction he supplicates, in blessing he forgets.” A penetrating diagnosis of the human condition: man is torn between greedy desire and fear, never at peace. Neither lack calms him, nor abundance fills him. This deep-seated anxiety is the soul's disease — for which the following verses give the remedy: prayer and giving.

versets.trad: fretful when misfortune touches him,
versets.commentaire: idhā massahu l-sharru jazūʿan (إذا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ جَزوعًا): when evil touches him, he panics. The first face of anxiety (halaʿ): in adversity, panic (jazaʿ), impatience, collapse. Qushayrī: “in affliction, he supplicates.” The unpurified man cannot bear misfortune: he struggles, laments, loses his footing. It is the opposite of beautiful patience (v. 5): instead of consenting and looking toward the One who afflicts, he revolts against the affliction. Panic in misfortune betrays a lack of trust; the remedy is the patience that surrenders to God.

mots.racine: م س س / ش ر ر

versets.trad: and grasping when good fortune touches him;
versets.commentaire: idhā massahu l-khayru manūʿan (إذا مَسَّهُ الخَيْرُ مَنوعًا): when good touches him, he grows niggardly. The second face of anxiety: in ease, withholding (manʿ), avarice. Qushayrī: “in blessing, he forgets.” The unpurified man, when comforted, does not share: he withholds, keeps, forgets the Giver. The two faces meet: panic in evil, avarice in good — never at peace, never generous. It is the portrait of the soul left to itself. To this twofold ill, the following verses oppose the twofold remedy: prayer (which calms the panic) and giving (which heals the avarice).

mots.racine: م س س / خ ي ر

mots.def: manūʿ: who withholds, refuses to give.

versets.trad: except those who pray,
versets.commentaire: illā l-muṣallīn (إلّا المُصَلّين): except the praying ones. The first exception to human anxiety, and the first remedy. Qushayrī gives a penetrating reading by wordplay on roots: the praying ones (muṣallīn) are “those who cling forever to the stations of need (mawāṭin al-iftiqār)” — for ṣalā evokes prayer, but also the supple joint, and the hollow of the loins. To pray is to stand in acknowledged need, bowed before God. The praying one escapes anxiety because he has handed over his fate to God: neither the panic of misfortune nor the avarice of good fortune seizes him, for his heart is anchored elsewhere.

versets.trad: those who are constant in their prayer,
versets.commentaire: ʿalā ṣalātihim dāʾimūn (عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ دائِمون): constant in their prayer. The prayer that heals is not occasional but permanent (dāʾim). It is not the isolated gesture that calms anxiety, but durable fidelity, the bond never broken with God. Constancy makes prayer not an act but a state: a permanent orientation of the heart. The one whose prayer is continuous keeps his heart ever turned toward its source; it is this permanence that preserves him from the two ills of anxiety. The regularity of the bond is what transforms.

versets.trad: those in whose wealth there is a recognized right
versets.commentaire: fī amwālihim ḥaqqun maʿlūm (في أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ مَعْلوم): in their wealth, a recognized right. The second remedy, against avarice (v. 21): to acknowledge that in one's fortune, a portion belongs by right to others. Qushayrī distinguishes three categories of givers: “those who prefer others with all their wealth — their fortune is for whoever asks; those who give and withhold — these are the ones the verse refers to; and those who see their hand as a hand of trust (yad al-amāna), not acting by their own choice but awaiting what they are directed to — these are the most accomplished.” To acknowledge the poor's right in one's wealth is to step out of avarice: what one gives is not a favour, but a due one restores.

mots.racine: ح ق ق / ع ل م
mots.def: ḥaqq maʿlūm: a determined portion, an established right.

versets.trad: for the beggar and the deprived,
versets.commentaire: li-l-sāʾili wa-l-maḥrūm (لِلسّائِلِ وَالمَحْروم): for the beggar and the deprived. Two figures of the poor: al-sāʾil, the one who asks, and al-maḥrūm, the one who, out of modesty, does not ask but is in need (the bashful one, says Qushayrī). The recognized right (v. 24) covers both: not only the one who holds out his hand, but the one who keeps silent out of dignity. True generosity does not stop at answering requests; it also seeks the hidden poor, the one who suffers in silence. To acknowledge the right of the maḥrūm is to have a heart attentive to discreet distress.

mots.def: maḥrūm: deprived, needy who does not ask.

versets.trad: those who hold the Day of Judgement to be true,
versets.commentaire: yuṣaddiqūna bi-yawmi l-dīn (يُصَدِّقونَ بِيَوْمِ الدّين): they hold the Day of Judgement to be true. Here is the root of all the preceding virtues. Why pray constantly (v. 23), acknowledge the poor's right (v. 24)? Because one believes in the account. Faith in the Day of Judgement is the foundation: whoever expects to render account lives differently. It is the exact opposite of the deniers who “expected no account” (s. 78, v. 27). To believe in the Day transforms the present life: one prays, one gives, one keeps watch — not by constraint, but because one knows that all counts.

mots.sens: those who hold to be true

mots.racine: ي و م / د ي ن

versets.ar: وَالَّذِينَ هُم مِّنْ عَذَابِ رَبِّهِم مُّشْفِقُونَ
versets.tr: wa-lladhīna hum min ʿadhābi rabbihim mushfiqūna
versets.trad: those who dread the chastisement of their Lord
versets.commentaire: min ʿadhābi rabbihim mushfiqūn (مِنْ عَذابِ رَبِّهِمْ مُشْفِقون): dreading the chastisement of their Lord. Ishfāq is a tender fear, mixed with apprehension and care, not servile terror. The praying one who believes in the Day (v. 26) keeps a vigilance of the heart: he never thinks himself safe. Qushayrī reported, on fear (s. 67, v. 12), that it removes all rest and keeps the heart on watch. This apprehension is no sterile anguish but a loving wakefulness: one fears to displease the One one loves. Reverent fear preserves from complacency and keeps the heart turned toward God.

mots.racine: ع ذ ب / ر ب ب
mots.sens: those who, of their Lord's chastisement

versets.trad: not deeming themselves safe from the chastisement of their Lord;
versets.commentaire: ghayru maʾmūn (غَيْرُ مَأْمون): from which one is not safe. God's chastisement is not a thing one can deem oneself definitively preserved from. This word forestalls presumption: none, however pious, should think himself in absolute safety. It is the balance of the way between fear (khawf) and hope (rajāʾ): hope without fear leads to complacency, fear without hope to despair. The praying one keeps both: he hopes for mercy without ever thinking himself safe. This maintained insecurity is a salutary humility: it keeps the heart from falling asleep.

mots.racine: ع ذ ب / ر ب ب

mots.sens: from which one is not safe

versets.trad: those who guard their chastity,
versets.commentaire: li-furūjihim ḥāfiẓūn (لِفُروجِهِمْ حافِظون): guardians of their chastity. A virtue of self-mastery, within the legitimate frame of marriage (v. 30). Qushayrī notes the spiritual subtlety: “their union with their wives is only for continence and the safeguarding of the soul, then for the desire of a progeny that remembers God; on condition of living with her in gentleness, neither dragging her to one's own whim nor enslaving her to one's desire.” Chastity is not the refusal of the bond but its just measure: the mastery of desire, and respect for the other. To guard one's purity is not to make the other the instrument of one's whim.

versets.ar: إِلَّا عَلَىٰ أَزْوَاجِهِمْ أَوْ مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَانُهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلُومِينَ
versets.tr: illā ʿalā azwājihim aw mā malakat aymānuhum fa-innahum ghayru malūmīna
versets.trad: except with their wives or those their right hands possess, for then they are not blameworthy,
versets.commentaire: fa-innahum ghayru malūmīn (فَإِنَّهُمْ غَيْرُ مَلومين): they are not blameworthy. Chastity is not the negation of desire but its right orientation, within the frame of marriage. Qushayrī had set the spiritual condition: that the union be for continence and the remembrance of God, in gentleness and respect, not for the enslavement of the other to one's whim. The legitimate bond, lived with this intention, is free of blame. The way is not the rejection of nature but its measured fulfilment: what is disorder outside the frame becomes, within the frame and the right intention, blameless.

mots.racine: م ل ك / ي م ن
mots.sens: or those their right hands possess
mots.def: mā malakat al-aymān: those under one's lawful charge.

mots.gram: inna + noun of negation + passive participle

versets.ar: فَمَنِ ابْتَغَىٰ وَرَاءَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْعَادُونَ
versets.tr: fa-mani btaghā warāʾa dhālika fa-ulāʾika humu l-ʿādūna
versets.trad: while those who seek beyond that are transgressors;
versets.commentaire: fa-ulāʾika humu l-ʿādūn (فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ العادون): those are the transgressors. Beyond the legitimate frame (v. 30), it is transgression (al-ʿudwān): to cross the bounds set by God. The ʿādī is the one who exceeds the measure, steps outside the limits. Self-mastery has a clear boundary; to cross it is to enter disorder. The verse draws the line: not to stifle desire, but to contain it in its just place. To covet beyond what is permitted is to make oneself a transgressor — not by arbitrary severity, but because boundless desire ravages the soul and others.

mots.racine: ب غ ي / و ر ي

versets.trad: those who keep the trusts confided to them and fulfil their pledges,
versets.commentaire: li-amānātihim wa-ʿahdihim rāʿūn (لِأَماناتِهِمْ وَعَهْدِهِمْ راعون): guardians of their trusts and their pledges. Qushayrī unfolds the spiritual sense of the amāna (entrusted deposit): “they keep the trusts that men confide to them, without betraying them. And the trusts of the Real that are with them: their outer limbs — which they do not sully with faults; the gnosis in their heart is a trust; the secrets between them and God are trusts; the obligations, the tawḥīd — all of this is a trust. The very love God deposited in the heart is a trust.” Everything, in man, is confided: the body, the heart, the secret, faith, love. Faithfulness to the trust is to misappropriate nothing of what is lent to us for God.

mots.racine: ع ه د / ر ع ي

versets.trad: those whose testimony is sound,
versets.commentaire: bi-shahādātihim qāʾimūn (بِشَهاداتِهِمْ قائِمون): firm in their testimonies. Qushayrī: “their testimony for God of His Oneness (waḥdāniyya), and among themselves for one another — they fulfil its rights.” Testimony has two faces: to attest the Oneness of God, and to speak truth among men. To stand firm in one's testimony is to betray the truth neither toward God (by associationism) nor toward men (by false witness). The uprightness of the witness is a faithfulness to the truth, which joins faithfulness to the trusts (v. 32): in everything, not to alter what is true.

versets.trad: those who keep up their prayer:
versets.commentaire: ʿalā ṣalātihim yuḥāfiẓūn (عَلى صَلاتِهِمْ يُحافِظون): they watch over their prayer. The list of virtues, opened by prayer (v. 22-23), closes on it. Prayer is both the beginning and the crown: it frames all the other virtues — giving, faith, fear, chastity, faithfulness, testimony. To watch over one's prayer (muḥāfaẓa) is to keep its quality, its time, the presence of the heart. Qushayrī reported of such men: “their sign is to prepare for death before it descends, as if they always stood ready to depart.” The guarded prayer keeps the heart awake, ready for the encounter.

mots.def: ḥāfaẓa: to watch over, keep carefully.

versets.trad: they will be honoured in Gardens.
versets.commentaire: ulāʾika fī jannātin mukramūn (أُولئِكَ في جَنّاتٍ مُكْرَمون): those, in Gardens, are honoured. The conclusion of the list: the praying ones with manifold virtues (v. 22-34) are honoured in the Garden. Mukramūn — honoured, given every regard: not only admitted, but treated with honour. The contrast with the anxious man (v. 19-21) is total: he, tossed between panic and avarice, had no peace; these, anchored in prayer and giving, find honour and rest. The reward is not only the Garden, but the honour of being there: to be treated by God as an honoured guest. Such is the end of one who has healed anxiety by prayer.

versets.trad: What is it with the disbelievers, that they hasten toward you with outstretched necks,
versets.commentaire: muhṭiʿīn (مُهْطِعين): hastening with craned necks, fixed stare. Qushayrī: “the ihṭāʿ is to fix something with the gaze without turning it away; this is how they behaved before the Prophet.” A return to the deniers: they rush toward the Prophet, eyes fixed, but not to believe — out of mocking curiosity or hostility. The question marvels at their attitude: why press forward thus, necks craned, when they refuse? The body draws near but the heart shuts. The fixed gaze upon the messenger is not listening, but frozen mockery.

mots.sens: what is it with the disbelievers
mots.def: mā li: what ails them that.

mots.racine: ق ب ل / ه ط ع
mots.sens: toward you, hastening with craned necks
mots.def: muhṭiʿ: who hastens with fixed stare.

versets.trad: in groups on the right and on the left?
versets.commentaire: ʿizīn (عِزين): in bands, in scattered groups. Qushayrī: “group by group, troop by troop.” The deniers gather around the Prophet, on the right and the left, in factions. The image speaks of the pressure of the hostile crowd, the stir of groups forming to mock or contest. But this number is deceptive (cf. s. 72, v. 24): the multitude that clusters does not make the truth. The scattered groups, dispersed right and left, contrast with the unity of the heart turned toward God. The hostile crowd scatters; the believer is unified.

mots.racine: ي م ن / ش م ل
mots.sens: on the right and on the left

versets.ar: أَيَطْمَعُ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ مِّنْهُمْ أَن يُدْخَلَ جَنَّةَ نَعِيمٍ
versets.tr: a-yaṭmaʿu kullu mriʾin minhum an yudkhala jannata naʿīmin
versets.trad: Does each of them hope to be admitted into a Garden of bliss?
versets.commentaire: a-yaṭmaʿu… an yudkhala jannata naʿīm (أَيَطْمَعُ أَنْ يُدْخَلَ جَنَّةَ نَعيم): does he covet to be admitted to the Garden of bliss? A question that unmasks the absurdity: would these mocking deniers (v. 36-37) nonetheless hope for the Garden? As in al-Qalam (s. 68, v. 38), it is the claim to want the reward without the deeds. Qushayrī: “no — you do not call them to it by that! It is not just: they are today deniers, and tomorrow they will be treated as they deserve.” To covet the Garden while denying faith and mocking the messenger is a contradiction: one does not reap what one has not sown. The bliss of the praying ones (v. 35) is not obtained by mere coveting.

mots.racine: ط م ع / م ر أ
د خ ل / ج ن ن / ن ع م
mots.sens: to be admitted to a Garden of bliss

versets.trad: By no means!... We created them from what they know.
versets.commentaire: khalaqnāhum mimmā yaʿlamūn (خَلَقْناهُمْ مِمّا يَعْلَمون): We created them from what they know. The kallā cuts off their claim (v. 38). The reminder is biting: they came from a lowly drop, like every man (cf. s. 77, v. 20). Let them remember their origin before coveting the Garden of bliss. The pride of thinking oneself deserving without works breaks upon the reminder of the baseness of the origin. One does not rise to the Garden by pretension, but by the acknowledgement of one's dependence and by the works of the heart. To remember whence one came disarms presumptuous coveting.

versets.ar: فَلَا أُقْسِمُ بِرَبِّ الْمَشَارِقِ وَالْمَغَارِبِ إِنَّا لَقَادِرُونَ
versets.tr: fa-lā uqsimu bi-rabbi l-mashāriqi wa-l-maghāribi innā la-qādirūna
versets.trad: I swear by the Lord of the Easts and the Wests: We have the power
versets.commentaire: bi-rabbi l-mashāriqi wa-l-maghārib (بِرَبِّ المَشارِقِ وَالمَغارِب): by the Lord of the Easts and the Wests. Qushayrī: “the lā is connective; the meaning: I swear.” God swears by His lordship over all risings and settings — over the whole extent of time and space. Innā la-qādirūn: We have the power. The oath introduces the affirmation of omnipotence (v. 41). The Lord of the Easts and the Wests, who orders the movement of the stars, has all power over His creatures. To swear by this universal lordship is to anchor the certainty of the divine power to replace the deniers.

mots.racine: ر ب ب / ش ر ق / غ ر ب
mots.sens: by the Lord of the Easts and the Wests

versets.ar: عَلَىٰ أَن نُّبَدِّلَ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمَسْبُوقِينَ
versets.tr: ʿalā an nubaddila khayran minhum wa-mā naḥnu bi-masbūqīna
versets.trad: to replace them with better than them, and none can outstrip Us.
versets.commentaire: an nubaddila khayran minhum (أَنْ نُبَدِّلَ خَيْرًا مِنْهُمْ): to replace them with better than them. Divine power affirms itself: if the deniers persist, God can replace them with others. Wa-mā naḥnu bi-masbūqīn: none can outstrip Us, escape Us. A reminder of the utter gratuitousness of existence (cf. s. 76, v. 28): none is indispensable. The survival of the deniers is no due but a respite; God could raise up a better humanity. This omnipotence does not crush but relativizes pride: those who think themselves necessary learn that they hang by a thread God can re-tie elsewhere.

mots.racine: ب د ل / خ ي ر

versets.ar: فَذَرْهُمْ يَخُوضُوا وَيَلْعَبُوا حَتَّىٰ يُلَاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي يُوعَدُونَ
versets.tr: fa-dharhum yakhūḍū wa-yalʿabū ḥattā yulāqū yawmahumu lladhī yūʿadūna
versets.trad: So leave them to wrangle and play until they meet the Day they are promised;
versets.commentaire: fa-dharhum yakhūḍū wa-yalʿabū (فَذَرْهُمْ يَخوضوا وَيَلْعَبوا): leave them to wade and play. Qushayrī: “the height of threat and blame toward them.” The Prophet is invited to exhaust himself over them no longer: let them go on with their vain wrangling (khawḍ) and their games until the promised Day. Surrender to God delivers the beloved from the weight of retaliation (cf. s. 73, v. 11). “Leave them” is not indifference but trust: God takes charge of what is beyond him. While they sink into the vain and the game, the believer keeps his heart turned toward the Day he knows is near. To each his occupation: they in the void, he in the watch.

mots.racine: و ذ ر / خ و ض / ل ع ب
mots.sens: leave them to wade and play
mots.def: wadhara: to leave; khāḍa: to wade; laʿiba: to play.

mots.racine: ل ق ي / و ع د
mots.sens: until they meet the Day they are promised
mots.def: lāqā: to meet; waʿada: to promise.

versets.ar: يَوْمَ يَخْرُجُونَ مِنَ الْأَجْدَاثِ سِرَاعًا كَأَنَّهُمْ إِلَىٰ نُصُبٍ يُوفِضُونَ
versets.tr: yawma yakhrujūna mina l-ajdāthi sirāʿan kaʾannahum ilā nuṣubin yūfiḍūna
versets.trad: the Day when they will come out of the graves in haste, as if racing toward goal-posts,
versets.commentaire: kaʾannahum ilā nuṣubin yūfiḍūn (كَأَنَّهُمْ إلى نُصُبٍ يوفِضون): as if racing toward standing-stones. Qushayrī: “as if they were hastening toward their idols; their haste to come out of the graves is likened to their haste, in this world, to run toward the standing-stones (nuṣub) to worship them.” A biting image: on the Day, the deniers rush from the grave as fast as they once rushed toward their idols. The same haste that bore them toward the false now bears them toward the account. Haste toward idols in life becomes haste toward judgement on the Day: one runs, at the end, in the direction one has run all one's life.

mots.racine: خ ر ج / ج د ث / س ر ع
mots.sens: the day they come out of the graves in haste

mots.racine: ن ص ب / و ف ض
mots.def: nuṣub: standing-stones, idols; awfaḍa: to hasten.

versets.ar: خَاشِعَةً أَبْصَارُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ذَٰلِكَ الْيَوْمُ الَّذِي كَانُوا يُوعَدُونَ
versets.tr: khāshiʿatan abṣāruhum tarhaquhum dhillatun dhālika l-yawmu lladhī kānū yūʿadūna
versets.trad: their eyes lowered, overcome with humiliation. That is the Day they were promised!
versets.commentaire: A closing verse: khāshiʿatan abṣāruhum tarhaquhum dhilla (خاشِعَةً أَبْصارُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّة) — eyes lowered, humiliation covering them. To the deniers who rushed forward with fixed, arrogant gaze (v. 36) now answers the lowered gaze of humiliation. The reversal is complete: the pride of the challenge (v. 1, “to demand the chastisement”) turns into abasement. Dhālika l-yawmu lladhī kānū yūʿadūn: this is the Day they were promised. What they thought far off (v. 6) and mocked has come. The sūra, opened on the challenge of a questioner, closes on the fulfilment of what he demanded — and on the humiliation of the one who judged far off what was near. The promised Day always keeps its promise.

mots.racine: خ ش ع / ب ص ر
ر ه ق / ذ ل ل
mots.def: rahiqa: to cover, overwhelm; dhilla: humiliation.

mots.racine: ي و م / و ع د
mots.sens: that is the Day they were promised
